title: Patriots might have shot at top free-agent receiver after Colts’ franchise-tag gamble
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Colts' decision to not franchise tag Alec Pierce makes him a free agent, attracting interest from the Patriots.
Alec Pierce, 25, led the NFL in yards per catch in 2024 and 2025, making him a top wideout free agent.
Patriots could consider cutting Stefon Diggs to afford Pierce's potential $25-30 million annual contract.

title: Vermont postal worker allegedly threw away mail she was supposed to deliver for months
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Natasha Morisseau, a Vermont postal worker, was cited for allegedly discarding mail she was to deliver in North Troy.
Police found discarded mail, including packages and holiday cards, during a search of a dumpster on Elm Street.
Morisseau faces charges of petty larceny and unlawful mischief; she is due in Vermont Superior Court on March 17.

title: Man in his 80s, dog dead in Foxborough fire
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: A man in his 80s and a dog died in a house fire on Granite Street, Foxborough, early Tuesday.
Firefighters from Foxborough, Mansfield, and Plainville contained the blaze in under an hour, but the house was heavily damaged.
The cause of the fire is under investigation; the man was the sole resident of the house.

title: Patriots players brought strippers, hookahs to celebrate at a Boston restaurant, its owner says. He’s now facing a license violation.
author: Molly Farrar
bullets: Patriots celebrated at Estella's with strippers, hookahs, violating liquor laws after winning AFC title.
Restaurant owner Helder Brandao blamed Patriots players for chaos, facing potential liquor license penalties.
Boston Licensing Board will decide on possible license suspension or modification following violations hearing.

title: Mass. students place No. 1 nationwide for AP scores, break all-time record
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Massachusetts students achieved the highest nationwide AP exam scores, with 35.8% scoring 3 or higher in 2025.
Gov. Maura Healey announced record-breaking AP exam success, highlighting increased accessibility and participation among students.
Massachusetts also had the highest participation of Black or African American students in AP exams, outside Washington D.C.

title: ‘Forced to take this pay raise’: Quincy City Councilors are collecting the raise they promised to repeal
author: Molly Farrar
bullets: New Quincy councilors receiving a raise they vowed to repeal due to legal restraints in state laws and the city charter.
Councilors plan to donate part or all of their $44,500 salary increase to local nonprofits and causes.
Previous Quincy City Council granted raises without public hearings, prompting new council members to push for a transparent process.

title: Rosenthal: Red Sox remain the ‘best fit’ as a trade suitor for Isaac Paredes
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Red Sox unlikely to trade for Isaac Paredes despite rumors, as their infield setup remains set after acquiring Caleb Durbin.
Ken Rosenthal suggests Jarren Duran could be trading chip for Paredes, but recent communication with Astros has been lacking.
Paredes hit 20 home runs in 102 games for Astros; his acquisition could address Red Sox's lineup power concerns.

title: New ESPN mock draft has Patriots choosing a ‘pass-rushing menace’ in first round
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: ESPN mock draft predicts Patriots will draft Texas A&M pass rusher Cashius Howell at No. 31.
Howell recorded 11.5 sacks last season, ranking seventh among FBS defensive linemen.
Patriots' need for pass rush improvement highlighted by team having sixth fewest sacks in regular season.

title: Salem State student’s killer convicted 40 years after her murder, prosecutors say
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: John Carey, 66, was convicted of first-degree murder for the 1986 killing of Salem State student Claire Gravel.
Gravel was found strangled in June 1986; new DNA evidence led to Carey's 2022 indictment.
Carey, serving a sentence for a 2008 attempted murder, faces life in prison without parole.

title: N.H. woman accused of civil rights violation after allegedly shooting at lost man because he was Black
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Diane Durgin, 67, faces charges in Weare, N.H., for allegedly shooting at a Black man who mistakenly drove to her property.
Prosecutors filed a civil complaint against Durgin, citing four Civil Rights Act violations and requesting a $5,000 fine per violation.
Durgin allegedly threatened the man, fired shots at his car, and made racially motivated statements, according to the New Hampshire AG.
